title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: This PR has multiple blockers that must be fixed before merge. The most critical is a `console.log` that dumps the full charge request body — including card PAN and CVC — to stdout, a direct PCI-DSS violation. There is also a silent catch block that swallows every coupon-application error, and a logic bug where `discount_amount` is always `0` in the response because `payload.amount` is overwritten before the subtraction. Beyond the blockers, the new file introduces a three-tier Strategy+Factory+abstract-class hierarchy for a six-row static lookup table (pure over-engineering), uses `as any` to add fields to a typed payment response, bleeds snake_case into a camelCase API, and ships zero unit tests for new money-handling math. Naming throughout the new code is poor (`doStuff`, `theData`, `tmp`, `result2`, `x`). The PR needs a meaningful second pass.

findings.title: Full charge body logged to stdout — PAN and CVC leak
findings.message: `JSON.stringify(theData)` serialises the entire request body before schema parsing, meaning `card.number` and `card.cvc` flow directly to stdout on every charge request. PCI-DSS prohibits plaintext PAN in logs and bans storing CVC anywhere, ever. The project ships a pino logger with a redaction allowlist specifically to prevent this — bypassing it with `console.log` defeats that control entirely. Delete this line. If you need to trace requests, log only `{ customerId: theData.customerId }` through the injected logger.

findings.title: User-supplied coupon code logged via console.log, bypasses pino redaction
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` writes attacker-controlled input directly to stdout, bypassing the pino redaction allowlist. Beyond the redaction bypass, this is a debug line that escaped review. Replace with `logger.warn({ couponCode }, 'unknown coupon code')` through the injected logger, or simply return the amount silently — an unknown code is not an error worth logging at all.

findings.title: Catch block silently swallows coupon-application errors
findings.message: `catch (e) { // TODO }` drops every exception from `doStuff` on the floor. If the coupon service throws — bad input, unexpected state, anything — the charge proceeds at the *original* undiscounted amount with no log, no error, and no signal to the client that the discount wasn't applied. The customer gets charged full price while believing they used a coupon. At minimum log and rethrow; better, let this propagate to the outer handler and return a meaningful 4xx/5xx.

findings.title: `discount_amount` is always 0 — logic bug
findings.message: Line 24 overwrites `payload.amount` with `finalAmount` (the discounted value). Line 36 then computes `payload.amount - finalAmount`, which is `finalAmount - finalAmount = 0` on every request. The coupon feature ships broken: the gateway receives the right discounted amount, but the client always sees `discount_amount: 0`. Fix: save the original amount before the try-block — `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` — and use that in the subtraction.

findings.title: Zero unit tests for new money-handling math
findings.message: The PR description explicitly marks 'Add unit tests for the coupon math' as a TODO follow-up, but money-handling branching logic should not merge without tests. There are at least eight code paths here (six coupon codes, unknown code, no code), rounding behaviour in `PercentageCouponStrategy`, and no guard against `FixedCouponStrategy` producing a negative amount (FIVEOFF on a $3 charge → -$200 cents sent to the gateway). These are exactly the cases that silently corrupt financial data in production. Tests must accompany the code, not follow it.

findings.title: `console.log` instead of the project's pino logger
findings.message: The codebase uses an injected pino logger throughout. `console.log` bypasses log levels, structured output, and the redaction allowlist. This is a debug line that must be removed, but if any logging survives here it must go through the injected logger: `logger.debug({ customerId: payload.customerId }, 'charge request received')`.

findings.title: `const response: any` and snake_case fields break the typed camelCase API contract
findings.message: `const response: any = transaction` turns off type checking for the payment response — precisely the object that carries financial data. Then `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` are snake_case while every other field in this API is camelCase, so any client expecting `discountAmount` gets `undefined`. Model the return type properly: `interface ChargeResponse extends Transaction { discountAmount: number; couponCode?: string; }` and drop the `any` cast.

findings.title: Poor names: `theData`, and `couponCode` never validated against the schema
findings.message: `theData` is `req.body` — call it that. More importantly, `couponCode` is extracted from the raw unvalidated body on line 16 before schema parsing. `chargeSchema` never sees it, so there is no length cap, no format check, and no `.trim()`. An attacker can send a 10 MB string as `couponCode`. Add `couponCode: z.string().min(1).max(32).optional()` to `chargeSchema` and read it from the validated `payload`.

findings.title: Exported entry-point named `doStuff`; variables `tmp`, `result2`, `x` throughout
findings.message: `doStuff` is a public, exported function at the boundary between the controller and the coupon logic — it deserves a name that says what it does: `applyCoupon(amount, couponCode)`. Inside the same file: `var tmp` (the resolved strategy), `let result2` (the discounted amount), and `var x` (the floating-point intermediate) all hide what the code means. `var` should be `const`. Name things for what they are.

findings.title: `FixedCouponStrategy` can produce a negative charge amount
findings.message: `return amount - this.amt` has no floor. Applying FIVEOFF (500 cents) to a $3.00 charge (300 cents) produces -200 — a negative amount that either explodes at the gateway or, worse, triggers a credit. Add `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amt)` and a corresponding test that verifies the floor.

findings.title: Strategy + Factory + abstract class for a six-row static lookup table
`AbstractCouponStrategy` is an interface wearing an abstract-class costume
findings.message: One abstract method, no shared state, no concrete behaviour. That is the definition of an interface. An abstract class here forces single inheritance for no reason and adds the implicit constraint that every coupon strategy must be a class. Use `interface CouponStrategy { apply(amount: number): number; }` — or, better, delete the abstraction entirely and inline the two implementations into a single function as described above.

findings.title: `as any` disables type safety on the payment response object
findings.message: `const response: any = transaction` is the compiler telling you the type doesn't fit — and the answer chosen here was to gag it. The right answer is to model the type: `interface ChargeResponse extends Transaction { discountAmount: number; couponCode?: string; }`. The `as any` cast on an auth/payment-critical return value is exactly the place you needed types most.

findings.title: Commented-out dead code should be deleted
findings.message: The `FREESHIP` block is commented out with a note that 'shipping not used yet.' This is fossil code — nobody knows if it's a plan, a rollback, or an abandoned idea. Git remembers it. Delete it; if FREESHIP ever ships, it gets added in that PR with a real commit message explaining why.

findings.title: `var` and `==` throughout new file violate project conventions
findings.message: `var x` on line 16 and `var tmp` on line 58 should be `const` — neither is reassigned. The entire `CouponHandlerFactory.build` method uses `==` instead of `===` for string comparisons (lines 34, 36, 38, 40, 42, 44); `code == 'WELCOME10'` works by accident because both sides are strings, but the house style is strict equality. The `eqeqeq` rule exists because `==` bites when types diverge.
